ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn class exists specifically for coffee-glazed pork ribs. Closest defensible anchor is the generic pork rib food product class. Coffee glaze and deep-frying preparation are not captured in the ontology.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, ribs — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for this deep-fried, coffee-glazed preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for coffee-glazed or Zi Char-style pork ribs. A00GJ (pork ribs) is the closest defensible match. Glaze and cooking method facets are not represented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for coffee pork ribs or equivalent Singaporean Zi Char dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Coffee Pork Ribs at a Singaporean Zi Char stall is approximately 200 g, comprising 3–4 bone-in rib pieces coated in a thick coffee-sugar-soy glaze, shared as part of a multi-dish family-style meal.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food — HPB Singapore / Zi Char dishes reference; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central pork ribs (cooked, braised) for fatty acid and micronutrient detail
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are adapted from HPB Singapore nutritional data for Zi Char pork rib dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for pork ribs, cooked (FDC ID 168169), adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption and coffee-sugar glaze contribution. Micronutrients (potassium, calcium, iron, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, vitamins) are carried from the input enriched record, which cites HPB Singapore, and cross-checked against USDA pork rib values for plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on sugar-glazed pork / mixed meat-and-sugar dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Coffee Pork Ribs. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on the high sugar content of the coffee-soy glaze (12.5 g sugar per 100 g), which drives glycemic response, moderated by the substantial protein (14.5 g) and fat (18.2 g) content of the deep-fried pork ribs, which slow gastric emptying. Reference anchor: sugar-glazed pork dishes and mixed protein-fat-sugar meals in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (58 × 31 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 18.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (320 mg per 100 g; 640 mg per 200 g serving) from soy sauce glaze, combined with high saturated fat from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Coffee Pork Ribs to occasional small portions (≤100 g) and request reduced-soy-sauce preparation where possible, as a single standard serving provides approximately 28% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (12.5 g per 100 g; 25 g per serving) from the caramelised coffee-sugar glaze, contributing to a medium GI of 58 and a glycemic load of approximately 18 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Coffee Pork Ribs due to the high free-sugar load in the glaze, which can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, portion size should not exceed 100 g and should be paired with high-fibre vegetables to blunt glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and saturated fat content (estimated 6.5 g saturated fat per 100 g); high sodium and high sugar further elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat Coffee Pork Ribs as an occasional indulgence only, given the combination of high saturated fat from deep-frying, high sodium from soy sauce, and high added sugar from the glaze, all of which are independently associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory potential from deep-fried fat and high refined sugar intake
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit consumption of Coffee Pork Ribs as deep-fried foods and high refined sugar intake are associated with increased systemic inflammation; balance with anti-inflammatory side dishes such as steamed leafy vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coffee Pork Ribs (咖啡排骨, Ka Fei Pai Gu) is a modern Singaporean Zi Char classic originating in Singapore's Chinese-influenced hawker and restaurant culture. The dish is prepared by marinating bone-in pork ribs, deep-frying them until golden and crispy, and then tossing them in a thick, sticky glaze made from instant coffee powder, sugar, and soy sauce. The glaze caramelises on the ribs during the final toss over high heat, producing a dark, shiny, intensely aromatic coating. The flavour profile is a distinctive combination of bitter coffee, sweet caramelised sugar, and savoury soy, with the richness of deep-fried pork providing a satisfying textural contrast between the crispy exterior and tender meat. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 285 kcal, 14.5 g protein, 18.2 g fat (including an estimated 6.5 g saturated fat from deep-frying), and 15.5 g carbohydrate of which 12.5 g is sugar from the glaze. Sodium is 320 mg per 100 g, making a standard 200 g serving a significant sodium contributor. The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium), with a glycemic load of approximately 18 per serving, driven primarily by the high free-sugar content of the glaze. Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension (high sodium), type 2 diabetes (high free sugar, medium-high glycemic load), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, sodium, and added sugar). It is best enjoyed as an occasional shared dish in a family-style Zi Char meal, balanced with steamed vegetables and plain rice. The dish is not suitable for halal diets due to pork content.

confidence_reason: Core macros are adapted from HPB Singapore Zi Char dish nutritional data and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central pork rib values. No exact HPB entry for Coffee Pork Ribs was located; values represent a well-supported adaptation. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA pork rib data adjusted for deep-frying. GI is an analogous estimate with no direct measurement available. Micronutrients are carried from the input enriched record and are plausible but not independently verified against a primary source entry for this specific dish.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, cholesterol, and moisture data for this specific Singaporean Zi Char preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore food composition database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched entry for coffee-glazed deep-fried pork ribs as of 2024. Saturated fat (6.5 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork ribs cooked data (FDC ID 168169) adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption. 2. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed trace/negligible — pork ribs with coffee-soy glaze contain no meaningful vitamin C; this is consistent with USDA pork rib data. 3. GI value of 58 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Coffee Pork Ribs exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. 4. The input record contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical '1 serving (200g)' entries); only one 200 g serving is retained. 5. Halal status is false as the dish contains pork. 6. The input source URL (https://www.hpb.gov.sg) is an approved source (HPB Singapore); the specific page title has been normalised to reflect the Zi Char dishes nutritional reference.
